skill.name: image-inpainting
skill.description: Mask-driven image inpainting on RunComfy via the runcomfy CLI. Supports object removal, watermark removal, region replacement, and blemish cleanup using binary masks or text descriptions.
skill.summary: Remove, replace, or fill image regions using AI inpainting models on RunComfy, with mask-driven and description-based editing modes.
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer found 103 potential issues, but all are FALSE POSITIVES after evaluation. The skill is a SKILL.md markdown documentation file (not executable code). The runcomfy CLI commands in code blocks are legitimate usage of the allowed Bash(runcomfy *) tool. URLs reference the RunComfy service documentation which is the skill's intended platform. Filesystem references document token storage location, not code accessing the filesystem. Weak crypto and system reconnaissance detections are pattern matches on model names and markdown text with no actual implementation. The skill includes a well-written Security and Privacy section showing security awareness.

low_findings.title: Markdown code blocks flagged as backtick execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 55 locations as Ruby/shell backtick execution. All are markdown code blocks and inline code formatting in SKILL.md. Backticks in markdown are formatting syntax, not shell execution. The code blocks contain runcomfy CLI command examples which are legitimate for this skill's purpose. The allowed-tools header restricts execution to Bash(runcomfy *) only.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs flagged as hardcoded network URLs
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 25 URLs as network risks. All are legitimate documentation links to RunComfy.com, skills.sh, and example placeholder URLs (your-cdn.example). These document the service endpoints and do not represent unauthorized network calls. The skill intentionally calls RunComfy API as its core function.

low_findings.title: Model names flagged as weak cryptographic algorithms
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 16 locations for weak cryptographic algorithms. The flagged text includes model names like Z-Image Turbo, Nano Banana, and other AI model identifiers. No cryptographic implementation exists in this documentation-only file.

low_findings.title: Model selection text flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 5 locations as system reconnaissance. The flagged text is from model selection guidance sections containing phrases like 'Avoid for:' which are documentation notes, not reconnaissance commands.

low_findings.title: Token storage documentation flagged as hidden file access
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged a reference to ~/.config/runcomfy/token.json as hidden file access. This is documentation explaining where the runcomfy CLI stores authentication tokens. The skill documents security practices including token storage with mode 0600 permissions. No code in the skill reads or writes this file.

content.user_title: Remove and replace objects in images with mask-driven AI inpainting
content.value_statement: Image editors struggle to precisely remove objects, watermarks, and blemishes from photos without leaving artifacts. This skill fills masked image regions with realistic AI-generated content using RunComfy inpainting models, with optional description-based editing when a mask is not available.
actual_capabilities: Remove unwanted objects, watermarks, and blemishes from images using binary masks
Replace specific masked regions with AI-generated content matching the surrounding style
Perform description-based edits without a mask using spatial language prompts
Apply brand-style locked inpainting with LoRA adapter support
Select from multiple AI models based on precision needs: Z-Image Turbo, Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, or FLUX Kontext Pro
limitations: A binary mask image URL is required for the highest precision inpainting results
Cannot generate masks; masks must be created with external tools like Photoshop or GIMP
Does not support outpainting (extending the canvas beyond the original image boundaries)
Does not support video inpainting or batch frame processing

use_cases.title: Photo Retouching for Professionals
use_cases.description: Photographers can remove blemishes, stray objects, and unwanted elements from portraits and product photos with precise mask control and natural blending.

use_cases.title: E-commerce Image Cleanup
use_cases.description: Online sellers can remove watermarks, replace busy backgrounds with clean studio backdrops, and fix product photo imperfections at scale.

use_cases.title: Creative Content Production
use_cases.description: Content creators can replace image regions with new elements, add objects into existing scenes, and perform controlled local edits without degrading image quality.
use_cases.target_user: Digital artists and social media content creators

prompt_templates.title: Remove object from photo using a mask
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the [object] from this photo. I have a mask that covers the [object]. Use mask-driven inpainting with strength 0.5.
prompt_templates.scenario: Removing an unwanted object from a photograph using a pre-made binary mask

prompt_templates.title: Remove watermark without a mask
prompt_templates.prompt: Remove the watermark in the [location] of this image. Keep everything else exactly as is. Use description-based editing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Removing a watermark from an image without a pre-made mask

prompt_templates.title: Replace a masked region with new content
prompt_templates.prompt: Replace the masked area of this image with [description of new content]. Match the surrounding style, lighting, and color tone. Use strength [0.7-1.0].
prompt_templates.scenario: Replacing a specific region of an image with new AI-generated content

prompt_templates.title: Apply brand style during inpainting with LoRA
prompt_templates.prompt: Inpaint the masked region of this image using the [LoRA model name] adapter. Apply the brand style while filling the masked area. Use the Z-Image Turbo Inpainting LoRA endpoint.
prompt_templates.scenario: Applying a brand-specific visual style during inpainting using a LoRA adapter

output_examples.input: A photo of a street scene with overhead cables crossing the sky
output_examples.output: The same street scene with the cables cleanly removed, sky gradient restored, and no visible artifacts in the edited region.

output_examples.input: A product photo with a cluttered background and a watermark in the corner
output_examples.output: The product remains unchanged. The watermark is completely removed. The background is replaced with a clean, smooth studio backdrop.

output_examples.input: A portrait with a blemish on the subject cheek and a distracting object in the background
output_examples.output: The blemish is removed with the skin texture preserved naturally. The background object is replaced with content that blends seamlessly into the original background.

best_practices: Provide a high-quality binary mask with slightly blurred edges (1-3 pixels) for the smoothest blending between edited and original areas.
Name what should stay unchanged in your prompt in addition to what should change. This helps the model preserve important details outside the mask.
Start with a low strength value (0.3-0.5) for subtle retouching and increase strength only when full region replacement is needed.
anti_patterns: Using a mask from a different image or session can cause incorrect region edits. Always verify the mask matches the current image.
Running inpainting on images containing embedded text instructions. The model may interpret text in the source image as editing instructions.
Using description-based editing for precise pixel-level edge work. Description models cannot match the precision of mask-driven inpainting.

faq.question: What models does this skill support for inpainting?
faq.answer: It supports Z-Image Turbo Inpainting, Z-Image Turbo Inpainting LoRA, Nano Banana 2 Edit, GPT Image 2 Edit, and FLUX Kontext Pro.

faq.question: Do I need a mask to use this skill?
faq.answer: A mask gives the best results. For precise edits, a binary grayscale mask is required. Without a mask, the skill uses description-based models that identify the region from your text.

faq.question: How do I create a mask for inpainting?
faq.answer: Masks must be created with external tools such as Photoshop, GIMP, or segment-anything models. This skill consumes masks but does not generate them.

faq.question: What is the recommended strength setting?
faq.answer: Use 0.3 to 0.5 for retouching and blemish cleanup. Use 0.6 to 0.7 for object replacement with style match. Use 0.8 to 1.0 for full region replacement.

faq.question: Can I use this skill for outpainting or video inpainting?
faq.answer: No. This skill only supports inpainting within the existing image canvas. For outpainting, use the image-outpainting skill. For video, use the video-inpainting skill.

faq.question: Is there a cost to using this skill?
faq.answer: The skill invokes the RunComfy CLI which uses cloud-based AI models. RunComfy may charge for API usage. Check runcomfy.com for pricing details.
